planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in well-drained to moderately moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; southern blackhaw is a tough, drought-tolerant native shrub or small tree of the southeastern US, producing clusters of dark blue-black, edible berries with a sweet, raisin-date flavor in fall — excellent wildlife food and an underused native food plant for zone 5–9 landscapes.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; very drought-tolerant once established — one of the most adaptable native shrubs for difficult sites. Viburnum rufidulum (southern blackhaw or rusty blackhaw) is a native deciduous shrub or small tree of the southeastern United States — found naturally in dry rocky woods, cedar glades, roadsides, and forest edges from Virginia south to Florida and west to Texas — and one of the most ecologically important and underused native fruiting shrubs for Gulf Coast wildlife gardens. The plant is distinguished from blackhaw (V. prunifolium) by its larger size, glossy dark-green leaves, and the distinctive rusty-red to reddish-brown fuzz (rufidulum = rusty) that covers the buds, new twigs, and leaf undersides. It produces large, flat-topped clusters of small white flowers in spring, followed by large clusters of 1/3-inch, dark blue-black drupes (berries) in fall. The berries have a remarkable, sweet, prune-raisin-like flavor when fully ripe after frost — uniquely pleasant among native berries, and used by indigenous southeastern peoples for food. Birds (especially mockingbirds, bluebirds, and cedar waxwings) consume the fruit enthusiastically. The plant provides outstanding fall color (glossy burgundy-red), excellent winter bark interest, and remarkable drought tolerance — among the most xeric-adapted native shrubs for alkaline, rocky, and dry sites in the Southeast.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Viburnum rufidulum
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Viburnums

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring or fall in well-drained to moderately moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to clay soil in shade to full sun; European grey sedge is one of the best groundcover sedges for dry shade — thriving in the dry, dense shade of live oaks and other large trees where few other plants survive — and naturalizing readily into low-maintenance sweeps of arching, fine-textured, deep-green foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, lean to moderately fertile soil in shade to partial shade; drought-tolerant once established in shade; mow or shear in late winter to refresh foliage before spring growth. Carex divulsa (European grey sedge or field sedge) is a clumping evergreen sedge native to the grasslands, roadsides, and dry woodlands of Europe and the Mediterranean region — but now naturalized across much of California, the Pacific Northwest, and parts of the Southeast, where it has been widely adopted in sustainable landscaping for its outstanding performance as a drought-tolerant, shade-tolerant groundcover. In dry shade — the notoriously difficult zone under established live oaks, magnolias, and other large tree canopies where irrigation is restricted and root competition is intense — grey sedge stands out as one of the most reliable and beautiful options: it forms dense, arching, fine-textured mounds of deep-green, grass-like foliage that remain attractive year-round with minimal care. It spreads slowly by seed and division to form a low-maintenance sweep. Unlike turf grass, grey sedge requires no mowing (though shearing in late winter refreshes its appearance), no fertilizer, and little water once established. The thin, arching leaf blades and graceful fountain form give a naturalistic, prairie-edge aesthetic. It is the backbone of California's 'dry shade garden' design movement, used extensively under native oaks in drought-tolerant home landscapes.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Drought-Tolerant Groundcovers
California Horticultural Society — Carex for Dry Shade

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; sapucaia or paradise nut is a large canopy tree of the Brazilian rainforest — closely related to Brazil nut (Bertholletia excelsa) — producing large, woody, barrel-shaped capsules (the 'monkey pots') containing edible nuts considered by many tasters to be superior to Brazil nuts in creaminess and flavor, with oil composition similar to macadamia oil.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; sapucaia requires genuinely tropical conditions and a long time to first fruiting — a tree for patient collectors in zone 11–12. Lecythis pisonis (sapucaia or paradise nut) is a massive canopy tree of the tropical rainforests of Brazil, Venezuela, and the Guianas — in the lecythis family (Lecythidaceae), closely related to Brazil nut (Bertholletia excelsa) and sharing its remarkable botanical story. The tree produces large, barrel-shaped, very heavy woody capsules (4–6 inches wide) that remain on the branches and open via a detachable 'lid' at the top — these are the famous 'monkey pots' or 'monkey cups': animals (monkeys, squirrels, agoutis) reach into the narrow opening to grab the nuts, but if they grab too many, their fist is too large to withdraw, trapping them. The nuts inside (5–40 per capsule) are considered by nut connoisseurs to be superior to Brazil nuts: milder, creamier, less waxy, with an exceptionally smooth texture and rich flavor, very high in healthy fats similar to macadamia oil composition. Sapucaia nuts are almost entirely unknown in commerce despite their exceptional quality, because they do not store as well as Brazil nuts (the high oil content means they go rancid quickly) — making them a fresh-nut delight available primarily near their source. A magnificent collector's tree for zone 11–12 tropical gardens.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Lecythis pisonis
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Paradise Nut
INPA (National Amazon Research Institute) — Lecythis pisonis

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; African breadfruit is a large, majestic West African tree — the largest member of the breadfruit/jackfruit family (Moraceae) — producing enormous globose fruits (12–36 inches across, up to 65 lbs) unlike common breadfruit: the edible part is the large starchy seeds rather than the flesh, and they are a major dietary staple in Nigeria, Cameroon, and Central Africa.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; a very large tree requiring considerable space — suitable primarily for large tropical properties in zone 11–12. Treculia africana (African breadfruit or ize) is one of the most ecologically and nutritionally important food trees of West and Central Africa — a massive, buttressed tropical tree native to the humid forests of Nigeria, Cameroon, Gabon, DRC, and Central Africa, producing the largest fruits of any species in the breadfruit/jackfruit family (Moraceae). The enormous globose fruits (12–36 inches across, weighing 10–65 lbs) erupt directly from the trunk and main branches (cauliflory) and are technically similar to jackfruit or breadfruit in structure, but used very differently: unlike common breadfruit (Artocarpus altilis, where the flesh is eaten) and jackfruit (where the flesh is the food), in African breadfruit, the edible product is the starchy, protein-rich seeds embedded in the fibrous flesh. The seeds are extracted, boiled or roasted, ground into flour, fermented (to make ugba or ukpaka — a traditional condiment in southeastern Nigeria), made into porridge, or pressed for oil. The fermented seed product (ukpaka) is a major flavor ingredient in Igbo cuisine, used similarly to cheese or miso as an aged, complex-flavored condiment. African breadfruit seeds are exceptionally protein-rich (21%) compared to other starchy staples. The tree is too large and slow-growing for most home gardens but represents one of the most important indigenous food forest trees of sub-Saharan Africa.
sources: FAO — Non-Wood Forest Products: Treculia africana
ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — African Indigenous Trees
Purdue University Center for New Crops — African Breadfruit

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; large sourplum is a thorny shrub or small tree from the savanna and bushveld regions of eastern and southern Africa — bearing single-seeded, oval, orange-red to scarlet, cherry-sized fruits with very sour, vitamin C-rich flesh and an almond-flavored kernel — one of the most widely used wild food fruits across sub-Saharan Africa.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; extremely drought-tolerant once established — adapted to seasonally dry African bushveld. Ximenia caffra (large sourplum or wild sourplum) is a thorny deciduous shrub or small tree from the dry savannas, bushveld, and woodland margins of eastern and southern Africa — native across South Africa, Zimbabwe, Mozambique, Malawi, Tanzania, and Kenya. The plant produces small (1-inch), oval, single-seeded drupes that ripen from green to bright orange-red to scarlet, with very tart, astringent, vitamin C-rich flesh surrounding a large seed — eaten fresh across rural sub-Saharan Africa (often with salt to balance the sourness), and made into jam, chutney, beer, and preserved sour fruit drinks. The seed kernel has an almond-like flavor and is pressed for Ximenia oil (sour plum oil), one of the most sought-after African cosmetic oils, with excellent skin-conditioning properties rich in long-chain polyunsaturated acids — increasingly used in high-end African cosmetics, hair oils, and skin care. Distinguished from the smaller X. americana (tallow wood) by its larger fruits, larger leaves, and preference for drier, more seasonal climates. As a water-wise shrub for zone 9–11 gardens, large sourplum is extremely tough and attractive, with white, almond-scented flowers in spring.
sources: South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI) — Ximenia caffra
Plants for a Future — Ximenia caffra
ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — Ximenia caffra

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, chalky or loamy soil in full sun; great pignut is a perennial carrot-family plant from the chalk grasslands and limestone slopes of Mediterranean Europe — producing edible, round, chestnut-like tubers 1–2 inches across with a sweet, hazelnut-chestnut flavor eaten raw or cooked — a wild food delicacy of European hedgerows and calcareous grasslands.

directions: Direct sow in early spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, chalky or loamy soil in full sun; harvest tubers in autumn when the feathery tops die back; difficult to cultivate commercially but rewarding in wild-style gardens. Bunium bulbocastanum (great pignut, earth chestnut, or ground chestnut) is a perennial carrot-family (Apiaceae) plant native to the chalk downs, limestone grasslands, and open woodland edges of southern and central Europe — from England and France south to the Mediterranean basin and Turkey. The plant produces feathery, finely-cut, carrot-like leaves above ground and a remarkable underground structure: a round, swollen tuber (1–2 inches diameter) with a thin brown skin and white, crisp, starchy flesh. The flavor is one of the most pleasant of any wild edible root: sweet, mild, and distinctly reminiscent of a blend of chestnut and hazelnut, with a crisp, juicy texture when fresh — eaten raw, cooked in stews, or roasted. 'Earth chestnut' and 'ground chestnut' both describe the remarkable resemblance of the flavor to chestnuts. In European wild food foraging, great pignut is one of the most highly regarded finds; in cultivation, it is challenging because it dislikes transplanting and requires lean, calcareous, well-drained soils. Distinct from Bunium persicum (black cumin, kala jeera), which is valued for its seeds rather than roots.
sources: Plants for a Future — Bunium bulbocastanum
Roger Phillips — Wild Food: A Unique Photographic Guide
Royal Botanic Garden Kew — Plants of the World Online
